page: Microsoft Azure D2_v2 is a general-purpose virtual machine belonging to the Dv2-series, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 2 dedicated physical cores on an x86_64 Intel Xeon E5-2673 v4 processor clocked at 2.3 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 7.0 GB of system memory, providing 3.5 GB per core, and is bundled with 102 GB of local storage. There are no GPUs included in this instance type.

Benchmark evaluations place the D2_v2 in the lower performance tiers. The Intel Xeon E5-2673 v4 processor delivers single-core and multi-core stress-ng scores that fall within the bottom 10% of tested hardware. Memory performance is similarly constrained, with large-block read and write bandwidths measured at 15.77 GB/sec and 11.57 GB/sec respectively, both ranking in the bottom 10%. Computational tasks such as software compilation with Clang and Passmark CPU tests also reflect these lower-tier capabilities.

Given its hardware profile and benchmark results, the D2_v2 is suited for lightweight, non-critical workloads. These include basic web serving, small-scale development or testing environments, and simple utility applications that do not require high memory bandwidth or intensive multi-threaded processing. The instance offers an entry-level resource density, making it a qualitative option for budget-conscious scenarios where high-performance throughput is not a requirement.
description: Microsoft Azure D2_v2 is a general-purpose virtual machine configured with 2 dedicated physical cores on an Intel Xeon E5-2673 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. It features 7.0 GB of RAM and 102 GB of local storage, with no GPU acceleration. Operating on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor, this x86_64 instance is designed for entry-level workloads. Benchmark data indicates lower-tier performance, with CPU and memory bandwidth metrics consistently placing in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested servers. Consequently, the D2_v2 is best suited for lightweight tasks such as basic web serving, small development environments, and low-intensity background processes where resource demands are minimal and cost-efficiency is prioritized over raw computational speed.
